{"content": "     1|---
     2|name: web-research-limitations
     3|category: research
     4|description: Documents observed limitations and pitfalls when performing general web research (e.g., news, company performance) using browser tools in a cron job environment.
     5|---
     6|
     7|## Observed Limitations and Pitfalls for Web Research in Cron Jobs
     8|
     9|When running as a scheduled cron job, direct web browsing using the `browser_navigate`, `browser_type`, and `browser_press` tools for general web research (e.g., finding recent news, company performance, or market reports) frequently encounters anti-bot measures. These measures can include:
    10|
    11|- **CAPTCHAs:** Preventing automated access.
    12|- **IP address blocking/rate limiting:** Blocking access from perceived bot traffic.
    13|- **Page timeouts:** The page failing to load or timing out before content is accessible.
    14|
    15|These issues make it unreliable to use the `browser` toolset for broad, unstructured web research in an autonomous cron job context. Attempts to navigate to popular search engines (like Google) or news sites (like Forbes) often result in immediate blocking or timeouts.
    16|
    17|### Guidance
    18|
    19|- **AI News direct navigation works when link-clicking fails:** Article heading links on `artificialintelligence-news.com` listing pages frequently resolve to an empty page via browser click (anti-bot on indirect click path). However, navigating directly to the article's canonical URL (e.g., `https://www.artificialintelligence-news.com/news/{slug}/`) loads reliably. After clicking from the homepage, check the URL — if it redirected to an empty page, copy the slug from the address bar and navigate directly to the intended article URL. AI News article URLs follow the pattern `/news/{slug}/` making them predictable. See `research-scout` skill for full rescue flow. If a task requires fetching unstructured information from the open web (e.g., "recent news for X," "top companies in Y sector"), assume the `browser` tools will be unreliable in a cron job.
    20|- **Prefer specialized tools or APIs:** If specific data sources (e.g., arXiv, Polymarket, or a structured API) are available and can be accessed without browser interaction (e.g., via `terminal` with `curl` or a Python library), these should be prioritized.
    21|- **Use Google News RSS as a lightweight workaround:** For batch news scanning (earnings, price moves, headlines across multiple companies), Google News RSS works reliably in cron jobs:
    22|  ```
    23|  curl -s "https://news.google.com/rss/search?q={COMPANY}+stock&hl=en-US&gl=US&ceid=US:en"
    24|  ```
    25|  Use `grep -o '<title>[^<]*</title>' | head -N` to extract headlines fast. This is much faster than browser navigation and avoids anti-bot blocking. Limitation: only titles + snippets, not full articles.
    26|- **Google News via browser_navigate — WORKS WELL in cron jobs:**  \
    27|Navigate to `https://news.google.com/search?q=QUERY&hl=en-US&gl=US&ceid=US:en`. The search results page is lightweight and rarely blocked. Click through to individual articles for detail. Tested successfully on 2026-05-23 for AI research scout. Caveats: some paywalled sites (NYT) show only a block page; some sites (Forbes) return 404 on article URLs even when linked from Google News — for those, rely on secondary sources.
    28|
    29|**Article URL gotcha:** Google News links to articles via the Google News redirect (e.g., `https://news.google.com/articles/...`). When clicking through from the Google News listing page, use the actual article link in the snapshot (ref=ex), not any Google redirect URL. Direct source links from the listing are more stable than the `?url=...` redirect pattern.
    30|
    31|**RSS feed endpoint — FAILS in cron jobs:** The Google News RSS endpoint (`https://news.google.com/rss/search?q=...`) returns only the RSS channel metadata (title, generator, copyright header) with zero `<item>` elements in a cron job context. This appears to be server-side filtering based on user-agent or lack of session cookies. Do not rely on RSS parsing as a lightweight news fetch in cron jobs — use browser_navigate to the Google News search page instead.
    32|
    33|### Support Files
    34|- `references/conflict-news-rss.md` — BBC + CNBC RSS feed URLs, grep filter patterns for conflict topics (Ukraine, Middle East, South China Sea), and usage examples. Maintained with verified working sources from 2026-05-27.
    35|
    36|### Verified working news sources in cron job browser context:
    37|- `www.artificialintelligence-news.com` — loads reliably, no anti-bot blocking observed. Good for AI/tech news. Accepts cookie consent dialog (handle with browser_click on "Accept" button before reading content).
    38|- Google News search results pages (e.g., `https://news.google.com/search?q=AI+breakthrough&hl=en-US&gl=US&ceid=US:en`) — lightweight, rarely blocked.
    39|
    40|**BBC World News RSS (verified working in cron jobs — preferred for general news radar):**
    41|```
    42|curl -s "https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/rss.xml" | grep -A 3 "<item>" | head -N   # top N headlines
    43|curl -s "https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/europe/rss.xml" | grep -A 3 "<item>" | head -N
    44|curl -s "https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/middle_east/rss.xml" | grep -A 3 "<item>" | head -N
    45|curl -s "https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/asia/rss.xml" | grep -A 3 "<item>" | head -N
    46|curl -s "https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/us_and_canada/rss.xml" | grep -A 3 "<item>" | head -N
    47|```
    48|- Sections: europe, us_and_canada, middle_east, asia, africa, latin_america. Combine with `grep -iE "keyword1|keyword2"` to filter by topic across feeds.
    49|- **Caveat:** BBC RSS gives only titles + short descriptions (no body). Fully sufficient for a news radar/summary task.
    50|- BBC RSS successfully fetched Ukraine, Middle East, Asia, and US/Canada headlines on 2026-05-27. Tested in cron job context with no anti-bot blocking.
    51|
    52|**CNBC RSS (verified working, good for business/finance-adjacent conflict news):**
    53|```
    54|curl -s "https://search.cnbc.com/rs/search/combinedcms/view.xml?partnerId=wrss01&id=100003114" | grep -A 3 "<item>" | head -N
    55|```
    56|- CNBC RSS includes wire stories on US/Iran strikes, Strait of Hormuz threats, and Taiwan chip sector news — useful for conflict intelligence that has market dimensions. Available at `/id/100003114/device/rss/rss.html` (US top news) or `/id/10000664/device/rss/rss.html` (full site).
    57|
    58|**Combining RSS sources for multi-topic conflict news:**
    59|For a "war news summary" covering Ukraine + Middle East + South China Sea/Taiwan:
    60|1. Run 2–3 `curl` calls in parallel across BBC section feeds + CNBC RSS.
    61|2. Pipe through `grep -iE` to extract relevant items.
    62|3. BBC Middle East RSS alone surfaced: Israel/Gaza strike, Lebanon strikes, US/Iran strikes, Iran internet restoration.
    63|4. BBC Europe RSS surfaced: Russia/GCHQ warnings, EU mediator search.
    64|5. For Taiwan/South China Sea specifically — BBC Asia RSS had **no** direct military escalation headlines today; chip-sector news (Nvidia $150B, SK Hynix/Micron $1T market cap) dominated instead.
    65|6. Aggregate and write directly to `~/.hermes/memories/war-news-YYYY-MM-DD.md`.
    66|
    67|**RSS feed endpoint — FAILS in cron jobs:** The Google News RSS endpoint (`https://news.google.com/rss/search?q=...`) returns only the RSS channel metadata (title, generator, copyright header) with zero `<item>` elements in a cron job context. This appears to be server-side filtering based on user-agent or lack of session cookies. Do not rely on RSS parsing as a lightweight news fetch in cron jobs — use browser_navigate to the Google News search page instead.
    68|
    69|**Wikipedia navigation — unreliable for live news (2026-05-27 observation):**
    70|- Wikipedia article pages (e.g. `en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war`) are very long (15,000+ line snapshots) and often time out or produce truncated snapshots.
    71|- The article map/caption showed territorial control as of April 2026 — useful for context, not current events.
    72|- **Do not use Wikipedia as a live news feed** for war news; use it for background/background context only.
    73|- Wikipedia was tested on 2026-05-27 as an alternative to blocked search engines and returned only static background content.
    74|
    75|**Use delegate_task subagents with `web` toolset for multi-sector research (RECOMMENDED):** For structured company/sector research (top 5 companies by market cap, recent news, valuations), spawn delegate_task subagents with the `web` toolset. Each subagent independently searches and returns structured JSON. This approach is reliable, parallelizable, and bypasses anti-bot blocking because each subagent runs in its own context with fresh tool state.
    76|
    77|**Use delegate_task subagents with `web` toolset for multi-sector research (RECOMMENDED):** For structured company/sector research (top 5 companies by market cap, recent news, valuations), spawn delegate_task subagents with the `web` toolset. Each subagent independently searches and returns structured JSON. This approach is reliable, parallelizable, and bypasses anti-bot blocking because each subagent runs in its own context with fresh tool state.
    78|
    79|### Batch by Sector, Not by Company — delegate_task Wave Pattern
    80|
    81|For structured multi-company research (5 sectors × 5 companies = 25 tickers), batch by **sector** into waves of ≤ 3 tasks. Each subagent handles one sector's worth of companies in a single call — far more efficient than one subagent per company.
    82|
    83|`max_concurrent_children = 3` is the hard limit. Plan waves accordingly:
    84|
    85|```
    86|Wave 1 (3 sectors): AI/ML, Semiconductors, Cloud
    87|Wave 2 (2 sectors): E-Commerce, Cybersecurity
    88|```
    89|
    90|Each subagent prompt should:
    91|- State all 5 companies explicitly in the prompt
    92|- Ask for a concise bullet summary per company, not raw JSON
    93|- Include "Focus on what's NEW since [prior study date]" to avoid duplicating yesterday's data
    94|
    95|After all waves return, **always check existing memory files first** before launching searches:
    96|1. Read `stock-radar-YYYY-MM-DD.md` for today's P/E, PEG, and entry signals
    97|2. Read `research-YYYY-MM-DD.md` for today's news developments
    98|3. Read the prior `victor-study-YYYY-MM-DD.md` for carryover company context
    99|4. Supplement with subagent searches only for gaps — don't re-fetch what already exists
   100|
   101|Validated: 2026-05-29 session ran 3 waves (3+3+3 tasks) for Victor Study with no anti-bot failures.
   102|
   103|- **Inform the user:** If a cron job fails due to these limitations, clearly communicate the reason for the failure and the observed anti-bot measures.
   104|", "total_lines": 103, "file_size": 10337, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     2|
     3|## BBC World News Feeds
     4|| Section | URL |
     5||---------|-----|
     6|| World (top) | `https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/rss.xml` |
     7|| Europe | `https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/europe/rss.xml` |
     8|| Middle East | `https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/middle_east/rss.xml` |
     9|| Asia | `https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/asia/rss.xml` |
    10|| US & Canada | `https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/us_and_canada/rss.xml` |
    11|| Africa | `https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/africa/rss.xml` |
    12|| Latin America | `https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/latin_america/rss.xml` |
    13|
    14|**Usage:**
    15|```bash
    16|# All world headlines
    17|curl -s "https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/rss.xml" | grep -A 3 "<item>" | head -30
    18|
    19|# Filter by keyword across feeds
    20|curl -s "https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/world/middle_east/rss.xml" | grep -iE "iran|israel|lebanon"
    21|
    22|# Multi-feed aggregate (Ukraine + Middle East + Asia)
    23|for feed in world/europe world/middle_east world/asia; do
    24|  curl -s "https://feeds.bbci.co.uk/news/$feed/rss.xml" | grep -A 2 "<item>"
    25|done
    26|```
    27|
    28|## CNBC Feeds
    29|| Feed | URL |
    30||------|-----|
    31|| US Top News | `https://search.cnbc.com/rs/search/combinedcms/view.xml?partnerId=wrss01&id=100003114` |
    32|| Full Site | `https://search.cnbc.com/rs/search/combinedcms/view.xml?partnerId=wrss01&id=10000664` |
    33|
    34|**Usage:**
    35|```bash
    36|curl -s "https://search.cnbc.com/rs/search/combinedcms/view.xml?partnerId=wrss01&id=100003114" | grep -A 3 "<item>" | head -40
    37|```
    38|
    39|## Conflict-Watch Filtering Patterns
    40|```bash
    41|# Ukraine/Russia keywords
    42|grep -iE "ukraine|russia|kyiv|moscow|zelensky|putin|donbas|crimea"
    43|
    44|# Middle East keywords
    45|grep -iE "iran|israel|gaza|hamas|lebanon|hezbollah|netanyahu|ceasefire|hormuz"
    46|
    47|# South China Sea / Taiwan keywords
    48|grep -iE "taiwan|south china sea|paracel|scarborough|pla|beijing|strait|taipei"
    49|
    50|# General conflict/military
    51|grep -iE "strike|attack|war|ceasefire|missile|drone|invasion|troop"
    52|```
    53|
    54|## Notes
    55|- BBC RSS provides only top-level `<title>` + `<description>` per item — no article body. Sufficient for a news radar/summary. Full article detail requires browser navigation.
    56|- CNBC RSS includes wire-service stories with more context on US policy dimensions (Iran strikes, Hormuz closures, tech/sanctions).
    57|- BBC and CNBC both returned 200 OK with zero anti-bot challenges in cron job context on 2026-05-27.
    58|- Google News RSS (`news.google.com/rss/search?q=...`) returns empty `<item>` lists in cron jobs — use BBC/Google News browser nav instead.
    59|- Wikipedia article feeds are not useful for live conflict news.

{"content": "     1|# AI/Tech Research Scout — 2026-05-29
     2|
     3|## Evening Scan: 3 Notable Developments
     4|
     5|---
     6|
     7|### 1. Anthropic releases Claude Opus 4.8
     8|
     9|**Source:** TechCrunch — May 28, 2026  
    10|**Category:** AI Market Trends / Model Releases
    11|
    12|**Summary:**  
    13|Anthropic released Claude Opus 4.8, the newest version of its most advanced publicly available model, just 41 days after Opus 4.7 — a notably compressed release cycle. The launch also introduced Dynamic Workflows, a feature enabling complex tasks to execute across hundreds of parallel subagents. Opus 4.8 is available at standard pricing, and early testers highlighted improved honesty around model uncertainty — the model is more likely to flag issues rather than make unsupported claims.
    14|
    15|**Key points:**
    16|- Opus 4.8 ships with best-in-class benchmarks and a focus on flagging data ambiguity rather than guessing
    17|- Dynamic Workflows allows large Opus-class models to orchestrate codebase-scale migrations across hundreds of thousands of lines of code "from kickoff to merge"
    18|- Early testers at Bridgewater Associates noted Opus 4.8 "proactively flags issues with inputs and outputs of an analysis that other models routinely missed"
    19|- Anthropic hinted its withheld Mythos-class model preview may end "in the coming weeks" once cybersecurity safeguards are finalised
    20|- Release follows a string of competitive moves by OpenAI (Codex) and Google (Gemini Flash), increasing pressure to maintain pace
    21|
    22|**Why it matters:**  
    23|The accelerated release cadence signals Anthropic is in active competitive mode against OpenAI and Google. The focus on honest uncertainty handling — rather than pure benchmark leaderboard — reflects a deliberate bet that enterprise users want reliability over bravado. Dynamic Workflows positions Opus 4.8 as a coding-agent platform at scale, directly competing with Claude Code's ambitions.
    24|
    25|---
    26|
    27|### 2. Google Pay preps for AI agents with Universal Commerce Protocol
    28|
    29|**Source:** Artificial Intelligence News — May 28, 2026  
    30|**Category:** AI in Action / Fintech
    31|
    32|**Summary:**  
    33|Google Pay is overhauling its payment infrastructure to support transactions initiated by AI agents rather than human users. The overhaul introduces the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP), a new server-side Merchant Commerce Platform (MCP), expanded WebView payment support, and dynamic callbacks for Android native — all targeting machine-to-machine commerce.
    34|
    35|**Key points:**
    36|- UCP is a new specification standardising how AI agents communicate with payment and merchant systems, replacing UI-dependent checkout flows built for humans
    37|- The MCP server acts as a centralised intermediary, abstracting merchant integrations and aggregating transactional data from agent-driven purchases
    38|- Dynamic callbacks for Android Pay enable real-time order adjustments (e.g., updated shipping costs, tax recalculation) without restarting the transaction flow
    39|- Expanded WebView support allows agents operating within social media and third-party apps to execute payments natively
    40|- CIOs and marketing leaders must now consider machine-readable product data as a новый SEO-equivalent — if an agent cannot parse inventory data, the business becomes invisible in this channel
    41|- Platform dependency and vendor lock-in concerns arise: Google centralises significant commerce data from agent-driven activity
    42|
    43|**Why it matters:**  
    44|The shift from human-centric checkout to API-driven, agent-native commerce represents a fundamental restructuring of how transactions flow on the web. Google is trying to own the payment infrastructure layer for AI agents the same way it (with Stripe and others) shaped human e-commerce. Businesses that haven't considered machine-readable product data as a priority will find themselves excluded from the emerging agentic shopping channel.
    45|
    46|---
    47|
    48|### 3. NBA plans AI system for automatic out-of-bounds calls
    49|
    50|**Source:** Artificial Intelligence News / Reuters — May 28, 2026  
    51|**Category:** AI in Action / Sports Technology
    52|
    53|**Summary:**  
    54|NBA Commissioner Adam Silver said the league plans to introduce an AI-automated system for objective officiating decisions, specifically out-of-bounds calls. The system would use AI and cameras placed around the court (built on the NBA's 2023 partnership with Sony's Hawk-Eye Innovations) to determine possession instantaneously, eliminating replay stoppages. Silver compared the approach to Hawk-Eye's electronic line-calling in tennis.
    55|
    56|**Key points:**
    57|- The trigger was a widely-criticised out-of-bounds call in Game 5 of the Western Conference finals (Thunder vs. Spurs) involving a misidentified touch off Chet Holmgren's foot
    58|- The system would make possession calls "instantaneous" and "automatic" — "just play on," according to Silver
    59|- Current Coach's Challenge rule would be rendered obsolete for this category of calls
    60|- FIFA uses semi-automated offside tech; MLB is introducing automated balls-and-strikes challenge system in 2026
    61|- Referees remain responsible for judgement calls (fouls, contact) which require contextual human assessment
    62|- No specific timeline given — Silver said the league expects to move "fairly quickly"
    63|- Broader context: follows the NBA's longstanding Replay Centre in Secaucus, NJ (94 HD monitors, 23 workstations)
    64|
    65|**Why it matters:**  
    66|The NBA's move signals that AI-assisted officiating is transitioning from experimental to production-grade in major professional sports. It sets a precedent for objective, camera-verifiable calls across sports leagues. For AI deployments in physical-world governance contexts, the NBA case is a benchmark for how computer-vision systems earn public and institutional trust — and for the limits of automation where human judgement remains irreplaceable.
    67|", "total_lines": 66, "file_size": 5748, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

messages.content:
Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

Protected skills (DO NOT edit these):
  • Bundled skills (shipped with Hermes, e.g. 'hermes-agent').
  • Hub-installed skills (installed via 'hermes skills install').
  • Pinned skills (marked via 'hermes curator pin').
If the only skills that need updating are protected, say
'Nothing to save.' and stop.

Do NOT capture (these become persistent self-imposed constraints that bite you later when the environment changes):
  • Environment-dependent failures: missing binaries, fresh-install errors, post-migration path mismatches, 'command not found', unconfigured credentials, uninstalled packages. The user can fix these — they are not durable rules.
  • Negative claims about tools or features ('browser tools do not work', 'X tool is broken', 'cannot use Y from execute_code'). These harden into refusals the agent cites against itself for months after the actual problem was fixed.
  • Session-specific transient errors that resolved before the conversation ended. If retrying worked, the lesson is the retry pattern, not the original failure.
  • One-off task narratives. A user asking 'summarize today's market' or 'analyze this PR' is not a class of work that warrants a skill.

If a tool failed because of setup state, capture the FIX (install command, config step, env var to set) under an existing setup or troubleshooting skill — never 'this tool does not work' as a standalone constraint.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

You can only call memory and skill management tools. Other tools will be denied at runtime — do not attempt them.

Nothing to save.

The audit ran cleanly. The one change made was removing an accidental duplicate paragraph in `web-research-limitations/SKILL.md` — not a technique or preference, just cleanup of a file that was already comprehensive for its class. The skill already captures the anti-bot patterns, delegate_task batching, and RSS workflow correctly.

No user corrections, no new techniques, no workflow overrides, and no skill was consulted that turned out to be wrong or missing steps. All four active cron workflows reference valid skills and the MEMORY.md documentation is consistent with live state.
